questions.q: The defining $C^*$-identity on a Banach $*$-algebra is:
questions.explain: The $C^*$-identity $\|a^*a\|=\|a\|^2$ is what makes $C^*$-algebras rigid: it forces the norm to be determined by the algebraic structure.

questions.q: Which element of a unital $C^*$-algebra is necessarily normal?
questions.explain: Self-adjoints satisfy $a^*a = a^2 = aa^*$ and unitaries satisfy $a^*a=1=aa^*$. Both are normal; normality includes strictly more than just these two classes.

questions.q: In the $C^*$-algebra $M_2(\mathbb{C})$, the operator norm of the self-adjoint matrix $\begin{pmatrix}3&0\\0&-4\end{pmatrix}$ equals:
questions.explain: For a self-adjoint operator the norm equals the spectral radius: $\|a\|=\max|\lambda_i|=\max(|3|,|-4|)=4$.

hard.q: Which statement is a correct counterexample showing the $C^*$-identity FAILS for a general Banach $*$-algebra?
choices: The disk algebra $A(\mathbb{D})$ with $f^*(z)=\overline{f(\bar z)}$ satisfies $\|f^*f\|=\|f\|^2$
On $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ with convolution and involution $f^*(n)=\overline{f(-n)}$, the element $f=\delta_1$ gives $\|f^*f\|=\|\delta_0\|=1$ while $\|f\|^2=1$, so it DOES hold here
The algebra $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ with the Frobenius norm $\|A\|_F=\sqrt{\mathrm{tr}(A^*A)}$ fails the $C^*$-identity: for $A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, $\|A^*A\|_F=\sqrt{3}\ne\|A\|_F^2=2$
hard.explain: $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ with the Frobenius norm is a Banach $*$-algebra but not a $C^*$-algebra. For $A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$: $A^*A=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&1\end{pmatrix}$, $\|A^*A\|_F=\sqrt{4}=2$, but $\|A\|_F^2=(\sqrt{2})^2=2$. Checking the operator norm instead: $\|A\|_{op}=\sqrt{2}$, $\|A^*A\|_{op}=2=(\sqrt{2})^2$, so the operator norm DOES satisfy the $C^*$-identity — this is why only the operator norm makes $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ a $C^*$-algebra.

hard.q: In $M_3(\mathbb{C})$, let $A$ be the diagonal matrix $\mathrm{diag}(2,-3,5)$. Using the $C^*$-identity, compute $\|A^*A\|$.
hard.explain: $A$ is self-adjoint, so $A^*A=A^2=\mathrm{diag}(4,9,25)$. Its operator norm equals the largest eigenvalue $25$. Alternatively, $\|A^*A\|=\|A\|^2=(\max|\lambda_i|)^2=5^2=25$.

hard.q: Which of the following correctly orders the steps in proving that a $C^*$-algebra has a unique $C^*$-norm (once the underlying Banach $*$-algebra structure is fixed)?
choices: Uniqueness follows from: (1) compute the spectral radius; (2) use $\|a\|^2=r(a^*a)$; (3) spectral radius is algebraically defined, so the norm is determined.
First show the norm is multiplicative, then invoke the Gelfand–Mazur theorem, then conclude uniqueness.
Construct the enveloping $C^*$-algebra and show all $C^*$-norms are submultiplicative.
Apply the Riesz representation theorem to each state, then use the GNS construction to extract the norm.
hard.explain: For any $C^*$-norm: $\|a\|^2=\|a^*a\|=r(a^*a)$ by the spectral radius formula for normal elements (and $a^*a$ is always self-adjoint, hence normal). Since $r(a^*a)$ depends only on the algebra structure (spectrum is algebraic), the norm is uniquely determined.

questions.q: For $a$ in a unital Banach algebra $A$, the spectrum $\sigma(a)$ is:
choices: a non-empty compact subset of $\mathbb{C}$ contained in $\{|\lambda|\le\|a\|\}$
always a finite set of eigenvalues
always a subset of the real line
questions.explain: Compactness: $\sigma(a)$ is closed (complement is open) and bounded by $\|a\|$. Non-emptiness: if $\sigma(a)=\emptyset$, the resolvent $(\lambda-a)^{-1}$ would be a bounded entire $A$-valued function, which by Liouville forces $a$ to be a scalar — but then $\sigma(a)=\{a\}\ne\emptyset$. Reality only holds for self-adjoint in a $C^*$-algebra.

questions.q: The spectral radius formula gives $r(a)=\lim_n\|a^n\|^{1/n}$. For the nilpotent $N=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\in M_2(\mathbb{C})$ (operator norm), compute $r(N)$.
questions.explain: $N^2=0$, so $\|N^n\|=0$ for $n\ge 2$, giving $r(N)=0$. Note $\|N\|=1\ne 0=r(N)$: the spectral radius strictly undercuts the norm. This is consistent with $\sigma(N)=\{0\}$.

questions.q: The resolvent map $\lambda\mapsto(a-\lambda)^{-1}$ on $\rho(a)=\mathbb{C}\setminus\sigma(a)$ is:
questions.explain: The resolvent identity $(a-\lambda)^{-1}-(a-\mu)^{-1}=(\lambda-\mu)(a-\lambda)^{-1}(a-\mu)^{-1}$ shows the quotient $((a-\lambda)^{-1}-(a-\mu)^{-1})/(\lambda-\mu)\to(a-\mu)^{-2}$ as $\lambda\to\mu$, so the resolvent is complex-differentiable with values in $A$.

hard.q: In the disk algebra $A(\mathbb{D})$ (continuous on $\overline{\mathbb{D}}$, holomorphic on $\mathbb{D}$) with the sup norm, take $a(z)=z$. Which statement is correct?
hard.hint: Characters on the disk algebra are evaluations at points of the closed disk.
hard.explain: Every character of $A(\mathbb{D})$ is $\mathrm{ev}_w$ for some $w\in\overline{\mathbb{D}}$, and $\sigma(a)=\{\chi(a):\chi\in\hat A\}=\{w:w\in\overline{\mathbb{D}}\}=\overline{\mathbb{D}}$. Hence $r(a)=\sup|\sigma(a)|=1=\|a\|_\infty$; this is a Banach algebra where $r=\|\cdot\|$ even without the $C^*$-identity.

hard.q: In $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ under convolution, $\delta_1$ satisfies $\|\delta_1\|_1=1$ and $\|\delta_1^n\|_1=1$ for every $n\ge 1$. Compute $r(\delta_1)$.
hard.hint: The Gelfand transform identifies $\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})$ with a subalgebra of $C(\mathbb{T})$; $\delta_1$ becomes $z\mapsto z$.
hard.explain: $r(\delta_1)=\lim_n\|\delta_1^n\|_1^{1/n}=\lim_n 1^{1/n}=1$. Under Gelfand $\delta_1\mapsto(z\mapsto z)$ on $\hat{\ell^1(\mathbb{Z})}=\mathbb{T}$, and $\sup|z|=1$ matches.

hard.q: Which statement captures the content of Gelfand's spectral radius formula beyond what submultiplicativity $\|a^n\|\le\|a\|^n$ gives?
choices: $r(a)\le\|a\|$ is trivial, but equality $r(a)=\lim_n\|a^n\|^{1/n}$ is a non-trivial complex-analytic identity using that $\sum \|a^n\|\lambda^{-n}$ converges outside $\sigma(a)$.
The formula only holds for normal elements.
The formula says $r(a)=\|a\|$ in every Banach algebra.
hard.hint: Think about when a power series $\sum a^n/\lambda^{n+1}$ for the resolvent converges.
hard.explain: Submultiplicativity gives $\|a^n\|^{1/n}\le\|a\|$, so $\limsup\|a^n\|^{1/n}\le\|a\|$. The real content is: the resolvent power series $\sum a^n/\lambda^{n+1}$ converges in the disk $|\lambda|>r(a)$ (by holomorphy of the resolvent outside $\sigma(a)$) and diverges inside, giving $\limsup\|a^n\|^{1/n}=r(a)$. The limit exists by Fekete-type submultiplicativity.

questions.q: For an element $a$ in a unital $C^*$-algebra, the spectrum $\sigma(a)$ is:
questions.explain: Compactness follows from $\sigma(a)\subseteq\overline{B(0,\|a\|)}$ closed; non-emptiness uses holomorphy of the resolvent $(\lambda-a)^{-1}$ combined with Liouville. Reality $\sigma(a)\subseteq\mathbb{R}$ only holds for self-adjoint $a$.

questions.q: The spectral radius formula says $r(a)=\lim_n \|a^n\|^{1/n}$. For a self-adjoint $a$ with $\|a\|=5$, compute $r(a)$.
questions.explain: For self-adjoint (hence normal) $a$ the $C^*$-identity gives $\|a^n\|=\|a\|^n$, so $r(a)=\|a\|=5$. Equivalently, the continuous functional calculus sends $a\mapsto\mathrm{id}$ on $\sigma(a)$, and $\|a\|=\sup_{\sigma(a)}|\lambda|=r(a)$.

questions.q: The continuous functional calculus for a normal element $a$ gives a $*$-isomorphism:
questions.explain: Gelfand–Naimark applied to the commutative $C^*$-algebra $C^*(a,1)$ generated by $a$ and the unit: its spectrum is $\sigma(a)$ and $f(a)$ is well-defined for any continuous $f$ on $\sigma(a)$. This is how one defines square roots, logarithms, spectral projections, etc.

hard.q: The spectrum of an element can depend on the ambient $C^*$-algebra. Which statement is correct?
choices: If $B\subseteq A$ are $C^*$-algebras and $b\in B$, then $\sigma_B(b)=\sigma_A(b)$ always
The spectrum can strictly shrink: $\sigma_B(b)\supseteq\sigma_A(b)$ with possible equality failing
The spectrum can strictly grow when passing to a larger algebra
Spectra are identical only when $A$ is commutative
hard.explain: A deep theorem of $C^*$-algebra theory: if $B$ is a $C^*$-subalgebra of $A$ (sharing the same unit or using approximate units appropriately), then $\sigma_B(b)=\sigma_A(b)$. This is spectral permanence, and it fails for general Banach algebras. It relies on the fact that invertibility of $b^*b$ or $1-b^*b$ passes between $B$ and $A$.

hard.q: Let $u$ be a unitary in a $C^*$-algebra ($u^*u=uu^*=1$). The spectral radius $r(u)$ equals:
Via the continuous functional calculus, if $a$ is self-adjoint with $\sigma(a)\subseteq[-1,1]$, one can form $b=\sqrt{1-a^2}$. Which properties does $b$ satisfy?
hard.explain: The function $t\mapsto\sqrt{1-t^2}$ is real-valued and non-negative on $[-1,1]$, so $b=\sqrt{1-a^2}$ is self-adjoint ($b^*=b$) and positive ($\sigma(b)\subseteq[0,1]$). The functional calculus is a $*$-homomorphism, so $b^2=1-a^2$, i.e., $a^2+b^2=1$. Also $ab=ba$ since both are functions of $a$.

questions.q: An element $a$ of a unital $C^*$-algebra is positive iff:
questions.explain: Equivalent characterizations: $a=b^*b$ for some $b$, or $a=a^*$ with non-negative spectrum. The set of positive elements $A_+$ is a closed convex cone generating the self-adjoint part.

questions.q: The partial order induced by positivity, $a\le b\iff b-a\ge 0$, restricted to $C(X)$ is:
choices: the pointwise order $f\le g\iff f(x)\le g(x)$ for all $x$
questions.explain: For $f\in C(X)$: $\sigma(f)=f(X)$, so $f\ge 0$ iff $f(x)\ge 0$ for all $x$. This is the classical pointwise cone; positivity in $C^*$-algebras is its noncommutative generalization (Löwner order in $B(H)$).

questions.q: In $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ consider $A=\begin{pmatrix}2&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$. How many of its eigenvalues lie in $[0,\infty)$? (needed to check whether $A$ is positive)
questions.explain: Eigenvalues are $2$ and $-1$; only $2\ge 0$, so one eigenvalue lies in $[0,\infty)$. Since not all eigenvalues are non-negative, $A$ is self-adjoint but NOT positive.

hard.q: Which is a valid counterexample showing a self-adjoint element need NOT be positive?
choices: $a=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}\in M_2(\mathbb{C})$: $a=a^*$ but $\sigma(a)=\{1,-1\}\not\subseteq[0,\infty)$, so $a$ is self-adjoint but NOT positive
Every self-adjoint element is automatically positive by the $C^*$-identity
In commutative $C^*$-algebras, self-adjoint elements are always positive
hard.hint: Find a self-adjoint element whose spectrum contains a negative number.
hard.explain: $\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}$ is self-adjoint (diagonal, real entries) but has eigenvalue $-1$, so $\sigma(a)\not\subseteq[0,\infty)$ and $a$ is not positive. Positivity requires $a=a^*$ AND $\sigma(a)\subseteq[0,\infty)$ — both conditions are necessary.

hard.q: Any self-adjoint $a$ can be decomposed as $a=a_+-a_-$ with $a_\pm\ge 0$ and $a_+a_-=0$ via the functional calculus. For $a=\mathrm{diag}(3,-4)$ in $M_2$, compute $\|a_-\|$.
hard.explain: Apply $t\mapsto t_-=\max(-t,0)$: $a_-=\mathrm{diag}(0,4)$, so $\|a_-\|=4$. Similarly $a_+=\mathrm{diag}(3,0)$, and $a_+a_-=0$ with $a=a_+-a_-$.

hard.q: Which of the following is NOT equivalent to $a\ge 0$ in a unital $C^*$-algebra?
hard.hint: The last option would make $-\tfrac12\cdot 1$ positive.
hard.explain: The first three are equivalent characterizations of positivity. The fourth fails: $a=-\tfrac12\cdot 1$ is self-adjoint with $\|a\|=\tfrac12\le 1$, but $\sigma(a)=\{-\tfrac12\}$ is negative, so $a$ is not positive.

questions.q: Gelfand duality identifies the category of commutative unital $C^*$-algebras with:
questions.explain: Unital commutative $C^*$-algebras $A$ correspond contravariantly to compact Hausdorff spaces via $A\mapsto \widehat{A}$ (characters) with inverse $X\mapsto C(X)$.

questions.q: For a character $\chi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ on a unital commutative $C^*$-algebra and $a\in A$, $\chi(a)$ always lies in:
questions.explain: If $\chi(a)=\lambda$ then $\chi(a-\lambda)=0$, so $a-\lambda$ is not invertible (it lies in $\ker\chi$, a maximal ideal). Hence $\lambda\in\sigma(a)$.

questions.q: Let $A=C(\{p_1,p_2,p_3\})\cong\mathbb{C}^3$. How many characters $\chi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ are there?
questions.explain: Each point $p_i$ gives an evaluation character $\mathrm{ev}_{p_i}$, and Gelfand duality says these exhaust the character space. So $|\widehat{A}|=|X|=3$.

hard.q: The Gelfand transform $\Gamma:A\to C(\widehat{A})$ sends $a\mapsto\hat{a}$ where $\hat{a}(\chi)=\chi(a)$. For $A=C([0,1])$, which statement about $\Gamma$ is correct?
choices: $\Gamma$ is a strict isomorphism with $\widehat{A}\cong[0,1]$ and $\Gamma$ is the identity
$\Gamma$ is injective but never surjective for infinite $X$
$\Gamma$ maps $A$ isometrically onto a proper subalgebra of $C(\widehat{A})$
hard.explain: For $A=C(X)$ with $X$ compact Hausdorff, the evaluation maps $\mathrm{ev}_x(f)=f(x)$ identify $\widehat{A}$ homeomorphically with $X$ (by Urysohn's lemma applied to separate points). Under this identification $\hat{f}=f$, so $\Gamma$ is the identity — a $*$-isometric isomorphism. Gelfand duality is an equivalence of categories, not just an injection.

hard.q: A commutative unital $C^*$-algebra $A$ is isomorphic to $C(X)$ for a unique (up to homeomorphism) compact Hausdorff space $X$. What goes wrong if we drop the requirement that $A$ is unital?
choices: Nothing; the theorem still gives $C(X)$ for some compact $X$
We get $C_0(X)$ (continuous functions vanishing at infinity) for a locally compact Hausdorff $X$ that need not be compact
The algebra is no longer a $C^*$-algebra
hard.explain: Without a unit, the Gelfand spectrum is locally compact but not necessarily compact. The non-unital version of Gelfand duality gives $A\cong C_0(X)$ for a locally compact Hausdorff space $X$. Compactness of $X$ is equivalent to $A$ being unital.

hard.q: Let $A=C([0,1])$ and $a(t)=t$. The Gelfand transform gives $\hat{a}=\mathrm{id}$ on $[0,1]\cong\widehat{A}$. Compute $\|\hat{a}\|_{\sup}$.
hard.explain: $\|\hat{a}\|_{\sup}=\sup_{t\in[0,1]}|t|=1$. The Gelfand transform is isometric, so this equals $\|a\|=\sup_{t}|t|=1$, consistent with spectral radius $r(a)=\|a\|=1$ for a normal element.

questions.q: In the noncommutative dictionary, a point of a classical space $X$ corresponds to:
choices: a character $\chi:A\to\mathbb{C}$ (commutative case); more generally a pure state
questions.explain: For $A=C(X)$, every character is $\mathrm{ev}_x$ and pure states are evaluations at points. For noncommutative $A$, pure states — the extreme points of $S(A)$ — generalize points even when $\hat A=\emptyset$.

questions.q: For an irrational $\theta\notin\mathbb{Q}$, the noncommutative torus $A_\theta$ (generated by unitaries $u,v$ with $vu=e^{2\pi i\theta}uv$) has how many characters $A_\theta\to\mathbb{C}$?
questions.explain: A character kills all commutators, but $[u,v]=(e^{2\pi i\theta}-1)uv\neq 0$ for irrational $\theta$. So $A_\theta$ has no characters — its classical spectrum is empty, though $A_\theta$ is a perfectly good 'noncommutative space'.

questions.q: In $A_\theta$ the relation $vu=e^{2\pi i\theta}uv$ uses the phase $e^{2\pi i\theta}$. Evaluate this phase at $\theta=1/4$ as $a+bi$.
questions.explain: $e^{2\pi i\cdot 1/4}=e^{i\pi/2}=\cos(\pi/2)+i\sin(\pi/2)=0+1\cdot i = i$.

hard.q: The noncommutative torus $A_\theta$ (irrational $\theta$) is a simple $C^*$-algebra. Which argument is the core of the proof?
choices: Simplicity follows because $A_\theta$ is commutative for all $\theta$
Any nonzero two-sided ideal $I\unlhd A_\theta$ must contain a nonzero projection, and by minimality of the unique trace $\tau$, $I$ contains the identity
Any ideal contains $u$ or $v$, but $u$ and $v$ generate all of $A_\theta$
hard.hint: Use the unique tracial state and positivity of $\tau$ on nonzero positive elements of $I$.
hard.explain: The standard proof: $A_\theta$ has a unique tracial state $\tau$. If $I$ is a nonzero closed ideal, one shows $\tau(p)>0$ for any nonzero positive $a\in I$ (using the Fourier expansion in $u,v$ and uniqueness of $\tau$). From this $1\in I$ by a projection argument, so $I=A_\theta$.

hard.q: For the noncommutative torus $A_{1/3}$, the relation is $vu=e^{2\pi i/3}uv$. Compute the real part of the phase $e^{2\pi i/3}$.
hard.explain: $e^{2\pi i/3}=\cos(2\pi/3)+i\sin(2\pi/3)=-\tfrac12+\tfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2}i$. The real part is $-\tfrac12=-0.5$.

hard.q: Which of the following is NOT part of the noncommutative geometry dictionary (Connes)?
choices: vector bundle on $X$  $\leftrightarrow$  finitely generated projective $A$-module
hard.hint: Points correspond to characters / pure states, not ideals.
hard.explain: Points correspond to characters (commutative case) or pure states (noncommutative case), not two-sided ideals. The dictionary is: bundles $\leftrightarrow$ projective modules (Serre–Swan); measures $\leftrightarrow$ states (Riesz–Markov generalized); maps $\leftrightarrow$ $*$-homs (Gelfand duality).

von-neumann-algebras.title: Von Neumann algebras and the double commutant

questions.q: A von Neumann algebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is characterized (von Neumann's double commutant theorem) as:
choices: a unital $*$-subalgebra equal to its own double commutant $M''$
any subalgebra closed in the weak-$*$ topology on $B(H)^*$
questions.explain: Von Neumann's double commutant theorem: a unital $*$-subalgebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is closed in the strong (equivalently weak) operator topology iff $M=M''$. Norm closure alone gives a $C^*$-algebra, which is strictly weaker.

questions.q: For $S\subseteq B(H)$, the commutant $S'$ is defined as:
choices: the set of all unitaries conjugating $S$
questions.explain: $S' = \{x\in B(H):[x,s]=0\ \forall s\in S\}$ is the set of operators commuting with everything in $S$. It is automatically a unital $*$-subalgebra closed in the weak operator topology — hence a von Neumann algebra.

questions.q: In $B(\mathbb{C}^2)=M_2(\mathbb{C})$, let $S=\{A\}$ with $A=\mathrm{diag}(1,2)$. The commutant $S'$ consists of all matrices commuting with $A$ — the diagonal matrices. What is $\dim_\mathbb{C} S'$?
questions.explain: A $2\times 2$ matrix $X=\begin{pmatrix}x&y\\ z&w\end{pmatrix}$ commutes with $\mathrm{diag}(1,2)$ iff $y=z=0$, i.e., $X$ is diagonal. The diagonal matrices in $M_2$ form a $2$-dimensional subspace.

hard.q: On $B(H)$ for $H$ infinite-dimensional, which topologies have the SAME closure of a convex (say bounded) $*$-subalgebra as the strong-operator topology?
hard.hint: Bipolar-type arguments; norm closure is strictly smaller.
hard.explain: For a convex subset of $B(H)$, weak and strong operator closures coincide (Kaplansky / Hahn–Banach). Ultraweak closure also agrees on norm-bounded convex sets. Norm closure is STRICTLY smaller than strong-operator closure in general — e.g., $K(H)$ is norm closed but its SOT closure in $B(H)$ is all of $B(H)$.

hard.q: In $M_3(\mathbb{C})$, let $M$ be the $*$-subalgebra of matrices of the form $\begin{pmatrix}a&0&0\\0&a&0\\0&0&b\end{pmatrix}$. Compute $M''$.
choices: $M$ itself (since $M$ is already a von Neumann algebra)
the scalar multiples of the identity
hard.hint: First compute $M'$; if $M$ is unital and weak-operator closed, $M''=M$.
hard.explain: $M'$ consists of matrices commuting with both $\mathrm{diag}(1,1,0)$ and $\mathrm{diag}(0,0,1)$, which is $M_2(\mathbb{C})\oplus\mathbb{C}$ on the block structure $\{e_1,e_2\}\oplus\{e_3\}$. Then $M''$ consists of matrices commuting with that; they must be scalar on the first block and scalar on the second — recovering exactly $M$. So $M=M''$, confirming $M$ is a von Neumann algebra (finite-dimensional, hence automatic).

hard.q: Which statement distinguishes the $C^*$-algebra structure from the von Neumann algebra structure on a $*$-subalgebra $A\subseteq B(H)$?
choices: $C^*$-algebras are norm closed; von Neumann algebras are WOT/SOT closed and contain spectral projections of all their normal elements.
$C^*$-algebras always contain the identity; von Neumann algebras never do.
Von Neumann algebras are always abelian; $C^*$-algebras never are.
$C^*$-algebras have a unique trace; von Neumann algebras never do.
hard.hint: Think about whether characteristic functions $\chi_E(a)$ live in $A$.
hard.explain: Norm closure vs. WOT closure is the main structural difference: a $C^*$-algebra is only required to be norm-closed, so $\chi_{[\alpha,\beta]}(a)$ for $a$ self-adjoint may fall outside it (characteristic functions are not norm-limits of continuous ones). A von Neumann algebra, being WOT-closed, contains all Borel-functional-calculus elements — in particular spectral projections.

questions.q: A von Neumann algebra $M\subseteq B(H)$ is called a factor if:
questions.explain: A factor has trivial center: $Z(M)=\mathbb{C}\cdot 1$. Every von Neumann algebra decomposes as a direct integral of factors; factors are the 'irreducible' building blocks. $B(H)$ is the prototypical type I factor.

questions.q: Match each factor with its Murray–von Neumann type:
questions.explain: Type I$_n$ = $M_n(\mathbb{C})$ (finite-dim matrices); Type I$_\infty$ = $B(H)$; Type II$_1$ admits a finite faithful normal trace, e.g., the hyperfinite $R$; Type III admits no semifinite trace, e.g., Powers factors $R_\lambda$ from III$_\lambda$.

questions.q: Which key data distinguishes the types?
choices: Whether $M$ contains a minimal (non-zero) projection and whether $M$ admits a semifinite normal trace
questions.explain: Type I $\iff$ minimal projections exist. Among types without minimal projections: semifinite normal trace exists $\iff$ type II; no semifinite trace $\iff$ type III. Traces are the key to separating II from III.

hard.q: Connes' classification of type III factors introduces parameters III$_\lambda$ for $\lambda\in[0,1]$. Which flow/invariant indexes this refinement?
choices: The Connes spectrum $S(M)$, a closed multiplicative subgroup of $\mathbb{R}_+^\times$
The type of a minimal projection
hard.hint: Modular theory gives a one-parameter group whose spectrum is invariant.
hard.explain: Connes' spectrum $S(M)\subseteq\mathbb{R}_+^\times$ is a closed multiplicative subgroup: III$_0$ has $S(M)=\{1\}$, III$_\lambda$ ($0<\lambda<1$) has $S(M)=\{0\}\cup\lambda^\mathbb{Z}$, III$_1$ has $S(M)=[0,\infty)$. This comes from Tomita–Takesaki modular theory and the modular flow $\sigma_t$.

hard.q: Which of the following is a valid example of a type II$_1$ factor?
choices: The group von Neumann algebra $L(\Gamma)$ of a countable ICC group $\Gamma$ (e.g., $\Gamma=F_2$ or $S_\infty$)
hard.hint: ICC = infinite conjugacy classes (except the identity).
hard.explain: For an ICC group $\Gamma$ (every non-trivial conjugacy class is infinite), the group von Neumann algebra $L(\Gamma)$ is a II$_1$ factor. Examples: $F_2$, $S_\infty=\bigcup S_n$. $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ is type I$_2$; $B(\ell^2)$ is type I$_\infty$; $L^\infty([0,1])$ has non-trivial center (itself), so not a factor.

hard.q: Murray and von Neumann proved that every factor has exactly one type from the list {I$_n$, I$_\infty$, II$_1$, II$_\infty$, III}. Which theorem does this classification rely on?
choices: Existence of a semifinite normal tracial weight and the comparison theorem for projections
hard.hint: You need to compare projections and find a trace.
hard.explain: The classification builds on: (1) comparison of projections $p\precsim q$ or $q\precsim p$ (always possible in a factor); (2) existence/uniqueness of a semifinite normal tracial weight when minimal projections fail; (3) types II vs III split by finite/non-existent trace. The spectral theorem and Riesz representation are used inside these constructions but not as the main classification engine.

questions.q: Projections $p,q\in M$ (von Neumann algebra) are Murray–von Neumann equivalent, $p\sim q$, iff:
choices: there exists a partial isometry $v\in M$ with $v^*v=p$, $vv^*=q$
$p$ and $q$ have the same operator norm
questions.explain: $p\sim q$ iff there is a partial isometry $v\in M$ (crucially within $M$) with $v^*v=p$, $vv^*=q$. Then $v$ implements an isomorphism between the ranges of $p$ and $q$ preserving the $M$-module structure.

questions.q: In $M_4(\mathbb{C})$ (a type I$_4$ factor), let $p=\mathrm{diag}(1,1,0,0)$ and $q=\mathrm{diag}(1,0,1,0)$. How many 1's do $p$ and $q$ have in common in their rank? (this is $\min(\mathrm{rank}(p),\mathrm{rank}(q))$; they are MvN-equivalent iff equal)
questions.explain: $\mathrm{rank}(p)=2=\mathrm{rank}(q)$, so $p\sim q$ in $M_4(\mathbb{C})$. In type I$_n$, MvN-equivalence is detected by rank: $p\sim q\iff\mathrm{tr}(p)=\mathrm{tr}(q)$, i.e., same rank.

questions.q: On a type II$_1$ factor $M$ with unique normalized trace $\tau$, the dimension function on projections satisfies:
questions.explain: For a II$_1$ factor, the trace $\tau$ restricts to a dimension function $d=\tau|_{\mathrm{Proj}(M)}:\mathrm{Proj}(M)\to[0,1]$. It is continuous, surjective, and $p\sim q\iff\tau(p)=\tau(q)$. This is one reason II$_1$ factors are sometimes called 'algebras of continuous dimension'.

hard.q: Arrange the steps in constructing the dimension function $d:\mathrm{Proj}(M)\to[0,1]$ on a II$_1$ factor $M$ with trace $\tau$:
items: Show the trace is unique up to scalar (uses simplicity of the factor and a comparison argument).
Verify $p\sim q\Rightarrow \tau(p)=\tau(q)$ via $v^*v=p$, $vv^*=q$ and the trace property.
Show every value in $[0,1]$ is attained by some projection (halving argument / orthogonal decomposition).
Conclude $d(p)=\tau(p)$ is a complete invariant of $p$ up to equivalence.
hard.hint: Start with uniqueness of the trace, then show the trace respects $\sim$, then achieve all values, then conclude.
hard.explain: (1) Uniqueness of $\tau$ is the first theorem; (2) the trace property $\tau(ab)=\tau(ba)$ gives $\tau(v^*v)=\tau(vv^*)$; (3) the halving lemma produces projections of any dyadic, hence real, trace in $[0,1]$; (4) combining gives $d$ as a complete invariant.

hard.q: In the hyperfinite II$_1$ factor $R=\overline{\bigcup_n M_{2^n}(\mathbb{C})}^{\mathrm{SOT}}$, what is the range of the dimension function?
choices: $[0,1]$ — every real value in $[0,1]$ is attained
hard.hint: SOT closure adds projections by limits; every real in $[0,1]$ arises as a limit of dyadics.
hard.explain: In finite $M_{2^n}$ only $k/2^n$ is achievable, but after SOT closure the dimension function is continuous and surjective onto $[0,1]$. Every real $r\in[0,1]$ is the trace of some projection in $R$.

hard.q: Which of the following fails in a general factor but holds in a II$_1$ factor?
choices: Every projection is MvN-equivalent to a subprojection of the identity
The dimension function is continuous with range $[0,1]$
Projections admit comparison $p\precsim q$ or $q\precsim p$
hard.hint: Only II$_1$ has a normalized trace giving a continuous $[0,1]$-valued dimension.
hard.explain: Continuity + range $[0,1]$ is special to type II$_1$. In type I$_n$ the range is $\{0,1/n,\ldots,1\}$ (discrete); in type II$_\infty$ the range is $[0,\infty]$; in type III every nonzero projection is equivalent to the identity (no dimension function). Comparison and MvN-subequivalence hold in every factor.

questions.q: A state on a unital $C^*$-algebra $A$ is a linear functional $\phi\colon A\to\mathbb{C}$ satisfying:
questions.explain: Positivity $\phi(a^*a)\ge 0$ plus normalization $\phi(1)=1$ forces $\|\phi\|=1$. States generalize probability measures: on commutative $A=C(X)$ they are exactly Borel probability measures by Riesz representation.

questions.q: The state space $S(A)$ is a convex subset of $A^*$. Its extreme points are called:
questions.explain: Pure states are the extreme points of $S(A)$: states $\phi$ that cannot be written as $\tfrac12(\phi_1+\phi_2)$ for distinct states $\phi_1,\phi_2$. On commutative $A$ pure states are characters; on $B(H)$ pure states are vector states $a\mapsto\langle a\xi,\xi\rangle$ for unit $\xi$.

questions.q: In $M_2(\mathbb{C})$ the trace state $\tau(a)=\tfrac12\mathrm{tr}(a)$ is a state. Compute $\tau\!\left(\begin{pmatrix}3&i\\-i&5\end{pmatrix}\right)$.
questions.explain: $\tfrac12\mathrm{tr}\begin{pmatrix}3&i\\-i&5\end{pmatrix}=\tfrac12(3+5)=4$. This matrix is self-adjoint with eigenvalues $4\pm\sqrt{2}>0$, so it's positive; a state sends positive elements to $\ge 0$ and sends $1$ to $1$, both verified here.

hard.q: A state $\phi$ on $A$ is pure iff:
choices: the GNS Hilbert space $H_\phi$ is finite-dimensional
hard.hint: Extreme points of $S(A)$ correspond to irreducible GNS reps via a convexity argument.
hard.explain: $\phi$ pure $\iff$ $\pi_\phi$ irreducible: if $\pi_\phi$ has a non-trivial invariant subspace, one decomposes $\phi$ as a convex combination of two distinct states (from projections in the commutant of $\pi_\phi$). Conversely, irreducibility forbids such decomposition.

hard.q: On $M_2(\mathbb{C})$, every state is $\phi_\rho(a)=\mathrm{tr}(\rho a)$ for a density matrix $\rho$ (positive, trace 1). Pure states correspond to rank-1 $\rho$. For the pure state $\phi_\rho$ with $\rho=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, compute $\phi_\rho\!\left(\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}\right)$.
hard.explain: $\rho a=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&0\end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\3&4\end{pmatrix}=\begin{pmatrix}7&2\\0&0\end{pmatrix}$, so $\mathrm{tr}(\rho a)=7$. Pure states on $M_n(\mathbb{C})$ are evaluations at unit vectors: $\phi_\rho(a)=\langle a e_1,e_1\rangle=a_{11}=7$.

hard.q: For $A=C(X)$ with $X$ compact Hausdorff, which statement about states is correct?
choices: $S(A)$ corresponds to Borel probability measures on $X$ via Riesz–Markov; pure states correspond to point masses $\delta_x$.
All states are pure in the commutative case.
Pure states on $C(X)$ are exactly the tracial states.
hard.hint: Riesz–Markov on $C(X)$ gives a bijection with Radon measures; normalize.
hard.explain: Riesz–Markov: positive linear functionals on $C(X)$ bijection with Radon measures on $X$; requiring $\phi(1)=1$ picks out probability measures. The extreme probability measures are point masses $\delta_x$, matching pure states $=$ characters $=$ evaluations in the commutative case.

questions.q: Arrange the five canonical steps of the GNS construction from a state $\omega$ on a unital $C^*$-algebra $A$:
items: Form the sesquilinear form $\langle a,b\rangle=\omega(b^*a)$ on $A$.
Let $N_\omega=\{a\in A:\omega(a^*a)=0\}$, a closed left ideal.
Pass to the quotient $A/N_\omega$ which carries a genuine inner product; complete to $H_\omega$.
questions.explain: Starting from the sesquilinear form $\langle a,b\rangle=\omega(b^*a)$, the null ideal $N_\omega$ is identified, the quotient completed to $H_\omega$, the action $\pi_\omega$ defined by left multiplication, and finally the cyclic vector $\Omega_\omega$ reproduces $\omega$ via the inner product.

questions.q: On $A=M_n(\mathbb{C})$ with the normalized trace $\tau(a)=\tfrac1n\mathrm{tr}(a)$, the GNS Hilbert space $H_\tau$ has dimension equal to:
questions.explain: For $A=M_3(\mathbb{C})$ (choose $n=3$ to get answer 9): the trace is faithful, so $N_\tau=0$, and $H_\tau=A$ completed in the inner product — but $A$ is already finite-dimensional, so $\dim H_\tau=\dim_\mathbb{C} M_3=9$. This is the Hilbert–Schmidt inner product picture. (For general $n$: $\dim H_\tau=n^2$.)

questions.q: The cyclic vector $\Omega_\omega$ satisfies which characterizing property?
questions.explain: $\Omega_\omega=[1+N_\omega]$ is cyclic: $\pi_\omega(A)\Omega_\omega=\{[a+N_\omega]:a\in A\}$ is dense in $H_\omega$ by construction. Cyclicity is what makes GNS reps the building blocks of all $*$-reps (via direct sum).

hard.q: Which properties does the GNS representation $\pi_\omega$ have?
choices: $\pi_\omega$ is irreducible iff $\omega$ is a pure state
hard.hint: Faithfulness fails when $\omega$ has a non-trivial left kernel.
hard.explain: $\pi_\omega$ is a cyclic $*$-rep (items A, D). Irreducibility $\iff$ purity (C). But $\pi_\omega$ need not be faithful: e.g., a character $\phi$ on $C(X)$ gives $\dim H_\phi=1$ and $\pi_\phi$ kills everything outside $\mathbb{C}\cdot 1$. Direct-summing over all states (universal rep) gives a faithful $*$-rep — the content of the noncommutative Gelfand–Naimark theorem $A\hookrightarrow B(H)$.

hard.q: For a character $\chi$ on a commutative $C^*$-algebra $C(X)$ (equivalently $\chi=\mathrm{ev}_x$), what is $\dim H_\chi$?
hard.hint: For $\chi=\mathrm{ev}_x$, $N_\chi=\{f:f(x)=0\}$, so the quotient is one-dimensional.
hard.explain: $N_\chi=\{f\in C(X):|f(x)|^2=0\}=\{f:f(x)=0\}$ is a maximal ideal of codimension 1, so $A/N_\chi\cong\mathbb{C}$ and $H_\chi=\mathbb{C}$. The corresponding rep is 1-dimensional, sending $f\mapsto f(x)$.

hard.q: The direct sum of GNS representations over all states — the universal representation $\pi_u=\bigoplus_\omega\pi_\omega$ — has which property?
choices: $\pi_u$ is faithful and isometric, embedding $A\hookrightarrow B(H_u)$
$\pi_u$ is trivial for $C^*$-algebras with no characters
hard.hint: Enough states separate points of $A$ — use positivity and the Hahn–Banach theorem.
hard.explain: Hahn–Banach + positivity gives: for each nonzero $a\in A$, some state $\omega$ has $\omega(a^*a)>0$, so $\pi_\omega(a)\ne 0$. Hence $\pi_u=\bigoplus\pi_\omega$ is faithful; it is automatically isometric (injective $*$-homs between $C^*$-algebras are isometric). This is the noncommutative Gelfand–Naimark theorem.

questions.q: An approximate unit in a (possibly non-unital) $C^*$-algebra $A$ is a net $(e_\lambda)_{\lambda\in\Lambda}$ with:
choices: $e_\lambda\ge 0$, $\|e_\lambda\|\le 1$, and $e_\lambda a\to a$, $a e_\lambda\to a$ for every $a\in A$
questions.explain: The standard definition: positive contractions forming an increasing net with $e_\lambda a\to a$. Every $C^*$-algebra (unital or not) admits one — construct via $e_\lambda=\lambda a^*a(1+\lambda a^*a)^{-1}$-type spectral calculus.

questions.q: In $c_0$ (sequences vanishing at infinity, non-unital), let $e_n$ be the characteristic sequence of $\{1,\ldots,n\}$ (so $e_n(k)=1$ for $k\le n$, $=0$ else). Compute $\|e_n\|$.
questions.explain: $\|e_n\|_\infty=\sup_k|e_n(k)|=1$, since at least one entry equals 1. The $e_n$ are projections in $c_0$ forming an approximate unit: for $f\in c_0$, $\|e_n f-f\|_\infty=\sup_{k>n}|f(k)|\to 0$ by vanishing at infinity.

questions.q: Why is an approximate unit important in non-unital $C^*$-algebra theory?
choices: It replaces the missing unit for purposes of functional calculus, GNS, tensor products, and ideals
questions.explain: Approximate units let non-unital $C^*$-algebra theory mirror unital theory: they are used to prove ideals are hereditary, to build tensor products, to extend states via $\phi(a)=\lim\phi(e_\lambda a e_\lambda)$, and to make the non-unital Gelfand transform work.

hard.q: Which $C^*$-algebra is NOT nuclear?
choices: The reduced group $C^*$-algebra $C^*_r(F_2)$ of the free group on two generators
hard.hint: Non-amenable groups give non-nuclear reduced group $C^*$-algebras.
hard.explain: $C^*_r(F_2)$ is NOT nuclear: $F_2$ is non-amenable, and the reduced $C^*$-algebra of a discrete group is nuclear iff the group is amenable (Lance). All commutative $C^*$-algebras are nuclear; $K(H)$ and $M_n(\mathbb{C})$ are nuclear (AF algebras are).

hard.q: A $C^*$-algebra $A$ is called amenable (in the Johnson sense) if every bounded derivation from $A$ into every dual Banach $A$-bimodule is inner. For $C^*$-algebras, amenability is equivalent to:
hard.hint: Connes–Haagerup: amenability = nuclearity for $C^*$-algebras.
hard.explain: A deep theorem (Connes for the injective factor case; Haagerup in general): for $C^*$-algebras, amenability in Johnson's Banach-algebra sense coincides with nuclearity (existence of completely positive approximations by factorizations through matrix algebras).

hard.q: Let $A=c_0$ and take the approximate unit $e_n=\chi_{\{1,\ldots,n\}}$. For $f\in c_0$ with $f(k)=1/k$, compute $\lim_{n\to\infty}\|e_n f-f\|_\infty$.
hard.hint: The limit is zero by the definition of approximate unit.
hard.explain: $(e_n f-f)(k)=0$ for $k\le n$ and $=-1/k$ for $k>n$, so $\|e_n f-f\|_\infty=\sup_{k>n}1/k=1/(n+1)\to 0$ as $n\to\infty$.
